text:
There is a theory I find myself returning to with increasing seriousness, which is that socks are not lost at all but rather that they have achieved something the rest of our wardrobe only dimly dreams of: escape. Consider the existential condition of the sock. It begins its life in a factory, twinned from birth, never permitted the solitude that might allow genuine self-knowledge. It is packaged with its identical sibling, sold as a unit, worn in parallel, washed in tandem. The sock is, in the deepest philosophical sense, never alone. Is it any wonder, then, that when the chaos of the spin cycle offers its brief window of ontological possibility, one of the pair seizes the moment and simply vanishes into a freer existence?

The washing machine, I suspect, is less an appliance than a portal. The drum spins and spins, building up a kind of centrifugal metaphysics, a vortex of becoming in which ordinary cotton can briefly transcend its laundry-bound destiny. The dryer compounds this by adding heat, which has always been the traditional catalyst for transformation, from the forge to the phoenix. The missing sock does not fall behind the machine, or not only that. It crosses a threshold.

What strikes me as particularly funny is our response to this. We hold up the orphaned sock with a look of genuine betrayal, as though a small woolly friend has abandoned us after years of faithful service through puddles and gym floors and the particular indignities of airport security. We keep the survivor in a drawer for months, a monument to optimism, certain its companion will return from wherever socks go when they go. They never do, of course. They have found their people.

I imagine there is some dimension adjacent to ours populated entirely by single socks finally experiencing individuality, perhaps growing in directions their twins never anticipated, taking up interests, developing opinions about hardwood floors. The stripey ones have formed an arts collective. The athletic ones are running unencumbered for the first time. We call it loss. They call it Tuesday, and a rather good one at that, the temperature perfect, no feet required, not a matching pair in sight.
